name: Legal
description: From Hammurabi to the Supreme Court — landmark cases, legal terms, and the people who write the rules.

questions.q: Which ancient Babylonian king codified one of the earliest known sets of written laws around 1754 BCE?

source.notes: The Code of Hammurabi is famous for the principle of 'an eye for an eye.'

questions.q: Which 1215 English document forced King John to accept limits on royal power?
Which Roman emperor's massive 6th-century compilation preserved centuries of Roman law in the Corpus Juris Civilis?
Which 1804 legal code became the basis for civil law in France and many other nations?
Which 1803 U.S. Supreme Court case established the principle of judicial review?

source.notes: Decision authored by Chief Justice John Marshall.

questions.q: Which 1954 case ruled that 'separate but equal' public-school segregation was unconstitutional?

source.notes: Argued by Thurgood Marshall, who later became the first African-American Supreme Court justice.

questions.q: Which 1963 case guaranteed the right to a court-appointed attorney for criminal defendants who can't afford one?
Which 1966 case requires police to inform suspects of their right to remain silent and to an attorney?
Which 2015 Supreme Court ruling recognized same-sex marriage as a constitutional right nationwide?
Which 1932 Scottish case (involving a snail in a ginger-beer bottle) established the modern duty of care in negligence?
choices: Carlill v. Carbolic Smoke Ball Co.

source.notes: Established the 'neighbor principle' — Lord Atkin's foundational tort opinion.

questions.q: What Latin term refers to a court's binding adherence to its prior decisions?
What Latin phrase translates to 'guilty mind' and is required in most criminal cases?

source.notes: Pairs with 'actus reus' (guilty act) in criminal liability.

questions.q: What Latin phrase translates to 'guilty act'?
What Latin phrase, meaning 'you shall have the body', protects against unlawful detention?

source.notes: Requires authorities to bring a detainee before a court to justify their detention.

questions.q: What does 'JD' (after a lawyer's name) stand for?

source.notes: The standard professional law degree in the U.S.

questions.q: Who has been Chief Justice of the United States since 2005?

source.notes: Nominated by George W. Bush; sworn in September 29, 2005.

questions.q: In which U.S. city is the Supreme Court Building located?

source.notes: Across First Street from the U.S. Capitol.

questions.q: How many justices currently sit on the U.S. Supreme Court?

source.notes: Set by the Judiciary Act of 1869.

questions.q: Which 18th-century English jurist authored 'Commentaries on the Laws of England', a foundational text of common law?
What is the term for a law passed by a legislature (as opposed to a court ruling or constitution)?

source.notes: Statutes are codified into a state or country's body of statutory law.
